ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sushi class; no specific aburi or tamago mayo variant exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers sushi as a category. No specific aburi trio mayo tamago sub-code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for aburi sushi or tamago sushi variant; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 180 g, equivalent to a plate of 3–4 aburi sushi pieces, as commonly served in Singapore Japanese restaurants and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sushi, with egg (tamago), rice-based; and Mayonnaise, regular; composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for 'Aburi Trio Mayo Tamago Sushi' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for sushi rice (FDC ID 169753), tamago egg omelette (FDC ID 173424 — egg, whole, cooked, omelet), and regular mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741), weighted to reflect a typical aburi sushi trio composition (~55% sushi rice, ~25% tamago, ~15% mayonnaise, ~5% nori/soy sauce). The input record values (energy 194.44 kcal, protein 5.56 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 19.44 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Flame-searing (aburi) does not materially alter macronutrient composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Sushi rice / white rice-based sushi
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aburi trio mayo tamago sushi. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based sushi (GI ~72, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Sushi rice is the dominant carbohydrate component (~55% by weight). Mayonnaise fat content may modestly attenuate the glycemic response, but the dominant GI driver remains the refined sushi rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 34.99) / 100 ≈ 25.1, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 19.44 × 1.8 = 34.99 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 250 mg/100 g with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: A single 180 g serving delivers approximately 450 mg sodium — nearly 20% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — with negligible potassium or fibre to counterbalance; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced soy sauce, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium items in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI sushi rice (GI ~72) with low dietary fibre (0.56 g/100 g) driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined sushi rice base produces a high glycemic load (~25 per serving), and the very low fibre content (0.56 g/100 g) provides no meaningful glucose-buffering effect; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to 1–2 pieces, pair with a fibre-rich or protein-rich side dish, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.94 g/100 g), trans fat (0.06 g/100 g), cholesterol (66.67 mg/100 g), and sodium (250 mg/100 g) from mayonnaise and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from mayonnaise, dietary cholesterol from tamago egg, trace trans fat, and moderate sodium presents a cumulative cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one serving per occasion and choose lower-sodium soy sauce alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aburi Trio Mayo Tamago Sushi is a Singapore-adapted Japanese sushi dish consisting of three pieces of nigiri or gunkan-style sushi, each featuring a different topping — with tamago (Japanese sweetened egg omelette) as the signature component — finished with Japanese mayonnaise and flame-seared (aburi) using a kitchen torch. The aburi technique caramelises the mayonnaise surface, producing a lightly smoky, creamy, and umami-rich flavour profile. The sushi rice base is seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, contributing a mildly sweet and tangy taste. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 194 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 10.0 g fat, and 19.4 g carbohydrate, with 250 mg sodium and 66.7 mg cholesterol. The high glycemic index (~72) from refined sushi rice makes this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The moderate sodium content warrants caution for those with hypertension. Saturated fat from mayonnaise and cholesterol from tamago egg are relevant for cardiovascular risk management. This dish is commonly sold in Singapore Japanese restaurants and food delivery platforms as a premium sushi set item.

What does tamago mean in sushi?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for sushi rice, tamago omelette, and mayonnaise, weighted by typical composition. Input values are consistent with this estimate and are retained. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based sushi. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this specific aburi trio mayo tamago sushi composite are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16 for this exact dish formulation. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace in sushi rice, tamago, and mayonnaise components per USDA FoodData Central; no vitamin-C-rich ingredient is present in this dish. 3. The input dietary_tag 'vegetarian' has been removed as tamago (egg) is present and the dish may contain fish-based toppings in the trio; the dish is not reliably vegetarian. 4. Ontology codes are approximate parent-class matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for aburi trio mayo tamago sushi. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice-based sushi from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available.
